serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried mackerel is one fish steak of approximately 120 g, as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and home meals.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Mackerel (Ikan tenggiri, fish steak, deep-fried) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Fried Mackerel (Ikan tenggiri, fish steak, deep-fried), CR ID F03020017. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15047) as a secondary approved reference for fatty acid breakdown only.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F03020017)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried mackerel contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F03020017). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI measurement is required or meaningful for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 993 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit fried mackerel prepared with heavy salt marinades; request grilled or steamed mackerel with minimal added salt as a lower-sodium alternative, and keep portion size to no more than 60 g if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar per 100 g; high protein (27.8 g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried mackerel has no glycaemic impact due to 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar; the high protein content supports satiety and blood glucose stability, but the deep-fried preparation adds fat calories that should be accounted for in total daily energy intake for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (993 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation offset the cardiovascular benefit of mackerel's omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While mackerel is naturally rich in cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, the very high sodium content (993 mg/100 g) and deep-frying method significantly increase cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer grilled or baked mackerel with minimal salt to retain omega-3 benefits while reducing sodium and oxidised fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 993 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite mackerel being an excellent omega-3 source with anti-inflammatory potential, the extreme sodium level and AGE-generating deep-frying preparation make this preparation unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis; grilled or steamed mackerel with minimal salt is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Mackerel (Ikan tenggiri goreng) is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian dish consisting of a Spanish mackerel (Scomberomorus commerson) fish steak that is marinated in salt and spices, then deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining moist and flaky inside. The dish is a staple in Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and home cooking, commonly served with steamed rice, sambal chilli, and sliced cucumber. The flesh of fried mackerel is firm, savoury, and rich in umami, with a slightly crispy skin from the frying process. Nutritionally, fried mackerel is an excellent source of high-quality protein (27.8 g/100 g) and provides moderate fat (8.7 g/100 g), including beneficial omega-3 fatty acids inherent to mackerel. Carbohydrate content is zero, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is very high at 993 mg/100 g due to salt used in the marinade, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The deep-frying preparation also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Calcium (40 mg/100 g) and iron (0.4 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts. This dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried mackerel?
Is fried mackerel good for diabetes?
Can I eat fried mackerel with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F03020017), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. The entry is an exact match for fried mackerel (ikan tenggiri, fish steak, deep-fried). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked mackerel as a secondary approved reference, clearly documented.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F03020017); mackerel contains no carbohydrates and the standard deep-frying preparation adds no carbohydrate-containing ingredients. Confirmed zero per HPB Food Composition Database. 2. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database; naturally zero as no sugar is present in the standard preparation. 3. dietary_fibre_g set to -1: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database reports dietary fibre as not available for this entry (source value -1); fish contains no dietary fibre and the value is not reported in the primary source. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this entry; not available in the primary source as of 2026. 5. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and water values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this food item. 6. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15047) as the closest approved secondary source; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 7. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g per HPB Food Composition Database); glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. 8. Cultural classification corrected from input: primary cuisine updated from 'western' to 'Singaporean / Southeast Asian' as ikan tenggiri goreng is a canonical Singaporean and Southeast Asian dish. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English (food_name), Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

This resolves both the g2 hard gate failure (gi_source not from approved organization) and the q2 source integrity failure. glycemic_index confirmed as JSON null (numeric null, not string) to resolve q4 numeric requirement.
